posts.name: Daniel Kim
posts.cooked:
2013 Rhys Horseshoe Chardonnay - Powerful aromatics of tangerine, candied ginger, orange peel, and some residual sulphur. Quite aromatic. Really bright acidity on the palate and the richness of the wine is quite well balanced by the liveliness on the acid.

2013 Rhys Bearwallow Pinot Noir - Historically I have not liked this. Seemed a bit under ripe and a bit weak on the mid palate but this had great forest floor, briar, dried rose petal aromas with wild strawberry. Quite elegant and delicate on the palate. Once the vines get a bit older I think this will be excellent although it’s quite good now.

2013 Rhys Santa Cruz Mountains - A blend of the Alpine and the Horseshoe with their excess grapes. Linear and pretty but not quite distinctive. Solid taken as itself but a weaker wine in the Rhys line-up.

2013 Rhys Family Farm - A bit stemmy at first but a nice sappy quality with medicinal spice and floral elements. A bit more tannic than the others and would recommend putting this away for a bit longer than the others.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: Daniel, thanks for the notes and good to see you there. I’m really liking the Rhys Chardonnays these days.

posts.name: Steve Brickley
posts.cooked: The Horseshoe chardonnay is a beauty - very light on its feet. I think the Santa Cruz Mountain PN stole the show for me. I am definitely going to get some of this PN for more frequent drinking (guessing $49 a bottle, but just my guess). Bearwallow isn’t a buy for me for same reasons above. I’ll skip FF because my emphasis is France these days, but I could see others liking it. If you get a chance to get to one of these events, by all means go – great food in a stunning setting with friendly Rhys folk all about.

I showed up rather late, about 2pm ish.

Quite a turnout.

I took notes, but in general I could tell the generosity and ripeness of the 2013 vintage. The 13 SC Mtn Pinot, while lacking some of the complexity and heft of the single vineyards, is a very good primer and entree into the Rhys catalog. It’s great to see the evolution of the Bearwallow vineyard from the first 08 vintage (with the original plantings) to now; the tannin profile has become less and less rustic with each vintage.

*unabashed fan.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
As usual, I showed up very early (like 10:01). I thought the wines all showed well and it was a gorgeous day up there. As in the Spring, the wines were poured outside, overlooking the Monte Bello ridge. The Horseshoe Chard was similar to previous vintages. I think it tends to show a little oak when young, this one seemed to show a bit less. As Carey indicated, the Pinots were forward and open, similar to the '12s but maybe even more forward. To me, the Bearwallow smelled like violets, red berries and spice, very pretty. Kevin indicated this is the first vintage to use their new plantings. As in the past, the appellation wine is labeled San Mateo County, even though this bottling could have been labeled SCM. The guy pouring said it ended up around 10% whole cluster. Very easy-going wine that should drink well young. The Family Farm was more structured and showed more WC. It also always strikes me as “earthy” rather than “rocky”, somehow conveys sense it comes from area with richer dirt.

-Al

[edited to correct Horseshoe vs Alpine Chardonnay]

posts.name: robert creth
posts.cooked: It was a lovely day and the wines were showing well. The Bearwallow was my fave. It was also fun fighting the wasps for the snacks. Got a case of the Alesia Chards.

posts.name: Brian King
posts.cooked: First time I’ve participated in the futures - coolest program I’ve seen. A mixed Pinot case of 500mls? AWESOME!

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Scott Fitzgerald:

2014 futures offering is live.

Damn. $259 for a six pack of 375ml Horseshoe Chardonnay, on futures? I do love their Horseshoe Chardonnay, but at that price, I don’t think I’m a buyer.

And I don’t say that to be critical or to complain – they may excellent wines, with expensive facilities and even more expensive real estate, have terrific customer service, and they probably have plenty of buyers at high prices, so they deserve to get what they can for their products – but I’m not sure how much longer I can stay with the pricing. Luckily for me, I’ve amassed quite a lot of Rhys over the last seven years or so.

More likely, I’ll keep buying, but just smaller quantities per regular offering.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: The mixed pinot sampler pack of 375s seems still like decent value. $449 for a dozen, so $37.41 each, plus tax and shipping. I just looked, and two years ago I ordered this same sampler pack, and it was $429, so not bad inflation there.

Chris Seiber:

The mixed pinot sampler pack of 375s seems still like decent value. $449 for a dozen, so $37.41 each, plus tax and shipping.

Just plus tax, since ground shipping is included as usual…

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: Ah, good to know. Thanks KBI.

posts.name: Kevin Harvey
posts.cooked:
Robert,
To expand on that a bit, starting with 2014 we will be offering two new Chardonnay bottlings.
Rhys Anderson Valley Chardonnay (made entirely with Bearwallow estate fruit) around $40
Rhys Bearwallow Vineyard Chardonnay $59

and an Anderson Valley appellation Pinot (also made entirely with Bearwallow estate fruit)
Rhys Anderson Valley Pinot Noir $45

With these offerings we hope to offer estate grown wine at Alesia prices.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Kevin, is there a future “will call” date available (i.e., spring)? The order page lists the Nov 1 date as the only option.

posts.name: Kevin Harvey
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

Kevin, is there a future “will call” date available (i.e., spring)? The order page lists the Nov 1 date as the only option.

Alan,
Yes we will have a pickup day this Spring (late March) for any pending orders. While we expect to have all the small bottle 2014s available at that time, we don’t intend to ship the larger bottle 2014s until Fall 2016.

posts.name: Gary Ahearn
posts.cooked: Has anyone (besides AG) had an opportunity to barrel taste the '14 Horseshoe and Alpine pinots? If so, what were your impressions, and what previous vintages are they similar to? Thanks.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I was sorry to miss the pick-up party. But I did taste a nice line up of the Rhys 2007s the other day at a harvest party in Napa, and can report that all the wines were showing beautifully, and are still very youthful. The 2007 Alpine Chardonnay was vibrant and expressive, and all the Pinot Noirs were beginning to develop some lovely complexity but still have a way to go before they reach anything like their peak. I didn’t take any notes worthy of their own thread, but people who own these should feel very happy about the positive evolution going on in bottle.
